title: Slab-built rectangular box with lid
subtitle: A small lidded container built from flat clay panels
excerpt: A rectangular air-dry clay box built from five flat slabs (four walls and a base) fitted with a recessed lid. The form is structural: the corners are mitred at 45 degrees and joined with score and slip. No kiln required. Finished size is approximately 100 mm x 70 mm x 60 mm.
sourceNotes: Slab construction is a standard hand-building method across many ceramic traditions. Technique derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection for historical lidded-box reference.

glossaryTerms.definition: Scratching joining surfaces with a needle tool and brushing them with clay slurry before pressing together. The standard join for any two pieces of clay.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join. Feels like stiff leather.
Fully air-dried but not fired. For air-dry clay, the finished structural state.
A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness and cut to shape.

content.text: A slab-built box is one of the most useful forms in hand-building. Five flat panels (four walls and a base) are joined at right angles to make a container, and a sixth
slab

forms the lid. The lid sits inside a recessed ledge so it does not slide off. This tutorial uses air-dry clay and requires no kiln. The finished box is for dry storage: buttons, seeds, small jewellery, hair clips. Do not use for anything wet.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.substitution: Paper clay (air-dry) for stronger corner joins

items.name: Fettling knife
items.substitution: Craft knife with a long blade

content.text: Preparation

Wedge 500 g of air-dry clay until smooth, then divide into three portions: 250 g for the walls, 100 g for the base and lid, and 50 g for the recessed ledge and patch-up. Prepare a cup of thick slurry by working a small piece of clay with water until it is the consistency of thick yoghurt.
Rolling the slabs

Place two slab guides on the board 200 mm apart. Roll the clay out between the guides using the rolling pin until the pin rides on the guides, producing a slab of even 8 mm thickness. Cut the following pieces with the fettling knife using a ruler as a straight-edge:
Two long walls: 100 mm x 60 mm each
Two short walls: 70 mm x 60 mm each (these slot between the long walls)
One base: 100 mm x 70 mm
One lid: 92 mm x 62 mm (slightly smaller than the box footprint)
Cover the pieces you are not joining immediately with plastic so they do not dry out before assembly.
Building the box
1. Join the first long wall to the base
Score and slip

the bottom edge of one long wall and the corresponding edge of the base. Press the wall upright onto the base edge. Run a finger along the inside seam to blend the join and reinforce it with a thin coil of fresh clay pressed into the corner. Wipe the seam smooth with the wooden rib.
2. Add the opposite long wall

Score and slip the bottom of the second long wall and press it onto the opposite base edge. Reinforce the inside corner as before. Stand the two walls parallel, 70 mm apart, held upright by propping against small clay blocks while you add the end walls.
3. Close the end walls

Score and slip the bottom and both side edges of each short wall and the corresponding faces on the base and long walls. Press each short wall into place, sealing the box. Reinforce all four internal corner seams with thin clay coils. Let the box stiffen on the board with the seams undisturbed for 30 minutes.
Adding the lid ledge
Roll four thin strips of clay, 6 mm wide and 8 mm thick. Once the box walls reach
leather-hard

(about one hour under plastic), score and slip the inside of the walls 6 mm down from the rim and press the strips flat against the inside walls all the way around. This creates a recessed ledge 6 mm below the rim. The lid rests on this ledge, held in by the walls. Smooth the ledge flush with the rib.
Fitting the lid

Place the lid slab on top of the ledge and check for fit. It should slip in without force and rest flat. Trim any overhang with the fettling knife. Add a small knob to the lid centre by scoring and slipping a ball of clay, or score a recessed grip line with the needle tool. Let the lid dry separately on the board so it does not bond to the box.
Drying

Dry the box slowly under a loose plastic tent for the first 24 hours so all panels shrink at the same rate. Corners dry faster than the middle of each slab; slow drying prevents the corners from cracking. Remove the plastic once all surfaces feel firm and leave the box to reach
bone-dry
over two to three days.
Finishing

Sand any rough surfaces with 220-grit sandpaper. Paint the outside with acrylics if desired, then seal with two coats of PVA or acrylic varnish, letting each coat dry fully before applying the next. Seal the lid separately. Do not varnish the inside of the ledge, which needs a slightly rough surface to keep the lid from sliding.
Variations
Use paper clay instead of plain air-dry. The cellulose fibres make the corner joints far less prone to cracking on larger boxes.
Press a texture stamp or lace into the outer wall slabs before assembly. The texture shows better on the flat faces than on assembled pieces.
Scale up to 150 mm x 100 mm x 80 mm for a tea-bag or card-holder box. Use 10 mm slabs for the larger size; 8 mm buckles on the longer walls.

items.cause: The corner seam was not reinforced with an inner coil, or the box dried too fast
items.fix: Patch with fresh slurry and a thin strip of clay pressed into the crack. Slow future drying under plastic for 24 hours

items.symptom: Walls bow inward or outward as they dry
items.cause: Slabs rolled to uneven thickness; thinner sections dry faster and pull
items.fix: Support the walls temporarily with crumpled paper inside until leather-hard. Use slab guides for even rolling next time

items.cause: Lid was left sitting in the box while the clay was still soft and bonded to the ledge
items.fix: Always dry the lid separately. If already stuck, run the needle tool gently around the lid edge to break the seal before the clay fully hardens

items.symptom: Base separates from the walls at the seam
items.cause: Score-and-slip join was insufficient on the base edge
items.fix: Re-score both faces, brush thick slurry, press together, and reinforce with an inner coil
